title: Trump's 100% Digital Tax Tariff Threat Escalates Transatlantic Trade Fight
subhead: A sweeping tariff ultimatum over digital services taxes opens a new US-Europe trade front, even as the legal tools to enforce it are running out. Meanwhile, the Fed's Warsh era begins with a break from forward guidance, the ECB insists inflation isn't going away, and oil markets digest the Iran deal's impact.

sections.heading: Trump Threatens 100% Tariff on Digital Services Tax Countries
sections.body:
President Trump on Friday posted on Truth Social that any country imposing a digital services tax on American companies would 'immediately be met with a 100% TARIFF on any and all Goods sent to the United States,' adding that the tariff would 'supersede Trade Deals made with the Country, whether implemented, signed, or not.'

The threat comes one day after EU member states gave final ratification to a transatlantic trade deal that caps most tariffs on EU exports at 15%. Digital taxes were explicitly excluded from that pact, and the issue has been a sticking point throughout negotiations. Nine EU countries — including France, Spain, Italy, Austria, Denmark, Hungary, Poland, and Portugal — already levy DSTs, while the UK has maintained its own 2% digital services tax since 2020, which raised more than £800m in 2024-25 according to UK Treasury data. Belgium, the Czech Republic, Latvia, Slovakia, and Slovenia have proposed similar measures.

The European Commission responded swiftly, with spokesperson Olof Gill calling unilateral measures 'unjustified' and stating the EU would 'respond swiftly and decisively to defend its rights and regulatory autonomy.' The commission characterized DSTs as non-discriminatory policies applied equally to all large companies regardless of origin.

But the threat faces a major legal problem. The Supreme Court's February 2026 ruling in Learning Resources, Inc. v. Trump ended the administration's ability to use IEEPA for tariffs, and the Section 122 authority Trump invoked as a replacement — currently imposing a global 15% surcharge — expires on July 24, just 26 days away. White House officials have indicated Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 is the intended mechanism, but that process requires a USTR investigation and cannot be triggered by presidential decree overnight. As the Tech Times analysis noted, 'no legal authority currently available to the president would permit a 100% tariff on any country.' Canada is the only country so far to drop its DST under US pressure, doing so last year.
sections.whyItMatters: The US-EU bilateral trade relationship accounts for nearly 30% of global goods and services trade and crossed $1.9 trillion last year. A 100% tariff would effectively sever that commerce — but the legal path to get there is unclear. The real risk is that the July 24 expiry of Section 122 tariffs creates a deadline that forces either legislative action or a new trade confrontation.

sections.heading: Fed's Warsh Era Begins: No Forward Guidance, No Dot Plot, No Precedent
sections.body:
New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh held rates steady at his first FOMC meeting on June 17, but the story was how he held them — and what he refused to do. Warsh declined to submit his own forward guidance or participate in the dot plot, told reporters the committee 'had a good family fight' about the decision, and announced five independent task forces covering AI, jobs, communication, productivity, and the balance sheet.

Warsh said he wants markets to react to 'incoming data' rather than Fed signaling, and characterized the shift as modernization: 'Private companies operate on real-time information, not echoes of history.' Nine FOMC members still projected a rate hike in the coming year, which moved markets. The policy statement itself was notably bare-bones compared to the Powell era — 'just gives you the facts,' Warsh said.

According to Reuters, a majority of economists now expect the Fed to hold rates through the end of 2026, defying market bets for hikes. Warsh's refusal to provide forward guidance means markets will have to price policy based on data releases rather than Fed projections — a fundamental change in how US monetary policy communicates with financial markets.
sections.whyItMatters: The end of forward guidance removes the single most important market-calming tool the Fed has used since 2008. With inflation running above target and nine FOMC members signaling hikes, the combination of hawkish sentiment and no forward guidance creates maximum uncertainty for bond and equity markets alike.

sections.heading: Iran Deal: Oil Sanctions Lifted, $300B Fund Proposed, Markets Assess Impact
sections.body:
The preliminary US-Iran peace agreement — signed around June 15-17 as a memorandum of understanding — has immediately reshaped oil market dynamics. The deal grants immediate waivers on US sanctions covering Iranian oil exports, according to multiple reports, and outlines a $300 billion redevelopment fund for Iranian reconstruction. Trump has insisted the US won't pay into the fund; it is privately financed and depends on Iran's compliance with the final agreement.

Iran has already seen a 'speedy rebound in oil exports' following the waivers, according to MSN reporting. The Strait of Hormuz has reopened to tanker traffic, easing the worst supply fears. But as a Reuters commentary noted, the Hormuz oil shock echoes the 1973 embargo in ways that matter beyond the immediate price — the conflict revealed how fragile the world's most critical oil chokepoint remains.

Critics have drawn unfavorable comparisons to the Obama-era JCPOA. Sen. Adam Schiff called the deal 'thorough capitulation,' citing the $300 billion figure and sanctions relief. Economist Steven Rattner noted the reconstruction fund is 'colossally higher' than Obama's. But the fund is not an upfront payment — it's a projected flow of private investment contingent on Iran meeting conditions, and experts warn that IRGC-controlled construction sectors create legal roadblocks under existing sanctions law.

Oil prices have declined from wartime highs but remain above pre-conflict levels, with gold also pulling back from its record above $4,500/oz in May to around $4,114/oz as of June 23. OPEC has cut its 2026 demand growth forecast to 970,000 bpd, and the market now projects a small surplus for 2026.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran deal removes the immediate geopolitical risk premium from oil but creates new uncertainties: how much Iranian crude hits the market, whether the $300B fund is real or aspirational, and what legal constraints remain on IRGC-linked entities. The oil market is shifting from crisis pricing to supply glut pricing.

sections.heading: ECB Hawks Circle: Schnabel and Lane Say Inflation Fight Isn't Over
sections.body:
ECB Executive Board member Isabel Schnabel warned on June 25 that inflation risks remain 'tilted to the upside' despite the decline in energy prices following the US-Iran deal. Schnabel argued that the conflict's damage to energy infrastructure is 'already baked into the system' and won't be unwound just because tankers can sail through Hormuz again. She highlighted the danger of unanchored inflation expectations — where businesses and consumers stop believing inflation will return to target — as a risk that justifies further rate increases.

ECB Chief Economist Philip Lane echoed the hawkish tone on June 16, saying the ECB would remain 'proactive' against high inflation even after the Iran deal. Governing Council member Martin Kocher warned that eurozone inflation would stay elevated despite the ceasefire, and Pierre Wunsch kept a July rate hike on the table if further evidence of persistent inflation emerges.

The coordinated hawkish messaging from multiple ECB voices signals that the Iran deal's energy-price relief has not changed the central bank's tightening trajectory. Schnabel's earlier statement from May 26 — that the ECB should raise rates in June 'even if a peace deal is struck' — has proven consistent with the bank's actions.
sections.whyItMatters: The ECB is the first major central bank to explicitly decouple its rate path from energy price relief, signaling that the post-Iran-deal inflation narrative is not a straight line from ceasefire to rate cuts. For euro-area borrowers and bond markets, the message is that higher rates persist longer than the geopolitical optimism would suggest.

sections.heading: BOJ Raises Rates to 31-Year High, Signals More Hikes Ahead
sections.body:
The Bank of Japan raised its benchmark rate to 1.0% from 0.75% on June 16, the highest level since 1995, in a 7-1 vote. The move had been widely expected after Bloomberg and Reuters reported the BOJ was set to hike. Governor Kazuo Ueda signaled the bank's readiness to continue raising borrowing costs, undeterred by global trade uncertainty.

A June 24 summary of the BOJ's policy debate showed that some board members called for faster rate hikes, citing mounting inflation risks — particularly from food prices and the pass-through from a weaker yen. Separately, BOJ sources told Reuters the bank would consider revising up its inflation forecast for the current fiscal year at its July meeting, reflecting persistent rises in rice and broader food costs.

The BOJ's tightening cycle is now the most aggressive since Japan exited its yield curve control framework, and the yen's partial recovery from multi-decade lows has been a key side effect. South Korea and Indonesia have also moved to defend their currencies — with Seoul vowing action against excessive speculation after the won hit its weakest level since 2009, and Jakarta tightening offshore derivatives trading. Bloomberg reports that Asia's central banks are pushing back against offshore forex speculation as a group, moving their currency fight offshore.
sections.whyItMatters: Japan's exit from ultra-loose monetary policy is a structural shift with global implications — it reverses the yen carry trade that has powered risk-taking worldwide for years. Combined with Asia-wide currency defense measures, the BOJ hike signals that the era of easy money isn't just ending in the West.

sections.heading: UK-Japan Sign £18B Investment Deal, G7 Critical Minerals Plan Faces Skepticism
sections.body:
The UK and Japan signed an £18 billion ($24.1 billion) investment partnership this week, with PM Keir Starmer hosting PM Sanae Takaichi at Downing Street. The deal includes up to £9 billion in Japanese investment for 5.9 GW of floating offshore wind projects in UK waters — enough to power 8 million homes — along with commitments in financial services, infrastructure, and technology. The agreement was finalized during the broader G7 summit in Évian-les-Bains, France.

At the same summit, the Trump administration's critical minerals pricing plan faced pushback from G7 allies. Reuters reported that negotiations for a Western trading bloc for minerals are 'stumbling over concerns about the plan's cost and governance.' A CSIS analysis noted the irony: the G7 was discussing critical minerals coordination at the same time the US-Iran deal opened access to Iran's mineral resources, complicating the supply diversification rationale. Industry divisions also run deep — mining companies are split on whether government-managed pricing would help or distort critical mineral supply chains.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK-Japan deal is one of the largest bilateral clean energy investments this year and signals that despite trade war tensions, allied nations are finding alternative channels for strategic investment. The G7 minerals stalemate, meanwhile, shows that Washington's industrial policy ambitions don't always align with allies' priorities — or with market participants'.

bottomLine: The global trade and monetary policy landscape is fragmenting along multiple fault lines this week. Trump's 100% digital tax tariff threat opens a new US-Europe front even as the legal authority to enforce it is about to expire. The Warsh Fed has stripped away forward guidance, leaving markets to price policy from data alone. The ECB and BOJ are both tightening despite very different inflation dynamics. And the Iran deal has shifted oil from crisis pricing to oversupply risk, while the legal and financial architecture behind its $300B reconstruction fund remains largely aspirational. The common thread: policymakers in every major capital are operating with less room for error and fewer tools than they had a year ago.
sources: Reuters, Bloomberg, Business Insider, Washington Post, Tech Times, IB Times, Mashable, CryptoBriefing, Moneycontrol, CSIS, Yahoo Finance, USA Today, Daily Business Group, Jang, Firstpost
